title: SOLID Principles
objectives: Remember what each letter stands for — with a simple real-life analogy
Recognize violations of each principle in real code
Understand how SOLID enables testable, maintainable code
Connect SOLID to Clean Architecture and dependency injection

sections.markdown:
**Imagine you're building furniture.**

- **S — Single Responsibility.** A chair has one job — sitting. A chair that also transforms into a table, a bed, and a coat rack is impressive but breaks constantly. Small tools do one thing well.
- **O — Open/Closed.** Your bookshelf lets you *add* books forever without rebuilding the shelf. It's open for extension (new books) but closed for modification (the shelf stays intact).
- **L — Liskov Substitution.** If someone hands you a chair, you should be able to sit on it — even if it's a beach chair or an office chair. A 'square' chair pretending to be a chair but breaking when you sit is a Liskov violation.
- **I — Interface Segregation.** Don't force a stool to implement a 'recline' method. Small, focused interfaces, not one giant 'furniture' interface.
- **D — Dependency Inversion.** Your lamp plugs into any wall socket — the lamp doesn't hard-code which specific power plant it uses. It depends on the *socket standard* (an abstraction).

**SOLID is five rules that make code less like a Jenga tower and more like Lego.**
**SOLID** is five object-oriented design principles — coined by Robert C. Martin — that reduce coupling and increase cohesion, making code easier to extend, test, and change without breaking.

rows: A class should have ONE reason to change
Open for extension, closed for modification
Subtypes must be usable as their base type
Many small interfaces beat one fat one

sections.markdown:
## S — Single Responsibility Principle (SRP)

> A class should have one — and only one — reason to change.

**The classic violation — the God class.**

```dart
class UserManager {
  User validate(Map input) { ... }        // validation
  User save(User u) { ... }               // database
  void sendWelcomeEmail(User u) { ... }   // email
  String toReport(User u) { ... }         // reporting
}
```

Four reasons to change: a validation rule change, a schema change, an email template change, a report format change. Each unrelated. Any change risks breaking the others.

**Fix:** Split into `UserValidator`, `UserRepository`, `WelcomeMailer`, `UserReporter`.

## O — Open/Closed Principle (OCP)

> Software entities should be **open for extension, closed for modification**.

When a new type appears, you should be able to add it *without* touching existing code.

## L — Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP)

> Anywhere the parent class works, a subclass must also work — without callers noticing or caring.

## I — Interface Segregation Principle (ISP)

> No class should be forced to depend on methods it doesn't use.

## D — Dependency Inversion Principle (DIP)

> High-level modules should not depend on low-level modules. Both should depend on **abstractions**.

**Dependency Inversion is the principle that unlocks:**
- **Dependency Injection** — hand collaborators to a class instead of it constructing its own.
- **Testability** — swap real DB for a fake in unit tests.
- **Clean Architecture** — domain layer depends on no framework.

Every major framework (Spring, Hilt, Riverpod, get_it) exists to make DIP practical at scale.

## A complete sample program — refactor a God class using all 5 principles

Before (bad):
## Common beginner mistakes

**Mistake 1: Making 'SOLID' the goal.**

SOLID is a means to an end — code that's easy to change safely. If applying a principle makes code worse (more abstract, harder to read, over-engineered), don't apply it. Principles serve you, not the other way around.

**Mistake 2: Over-applying DIP — an interface for every class.**

Creating `IUserService` for `UserService` when there's only ever one implementation is ceremony without benefit. Add abstractions when you'll swap implementations (testing, multi-env, migration).

**Mistake 3: Splitting classes so small nothing does anything.**

One class per method is not SRP — it's fragmentation. SRP means *one reason to change*, not *one line of code*.
## Best practices
**1. Start with SRP + DIP — they carry 80% of the benefit.** The others fall out naturally as your code grows.
**2. Constructor injection is almost always the right form.** Fields are explicit, mutability is discouraged, and testability is automatic.
**3. Watch for LSP violations in your inheritance.** If a subclass surprises callers, refactor to composition or a shared interface.
**4. Small interfaces > big ones.** If most implementers throw NotImplementedException on a method, that method probably belongs on a separate interface.
## Practice programs

**Practice 1 (Easy) — Refactor a God class.** Take a 200-line class that does 5 things. Split by SRP. Count how many were on 1 reason to change vs 5.

**Why:** You'll see SRP change your code fundamentally.

**Practice 2 (Medium) — Add a feature via OCP.** Have a code that switches on a `type` field to route behavior. Refactor to polymorphic dispatch. Add a new type — prove you touched no existing code.

**Why:** OCP becomes concrete after doing this once.

**Practice 3 (Hard) — Introduce DIP so a service is testable.** Take a service that news up its own DB connection. Inject an interface. Write a fake in a unit test that runs without a real DB.

**Why:** This is the pattern under every clean-architecture app.

**Practice 4 (Expert) — Fix an LSP violation.** Design a `PenguinShape extends Bird`. Notice `PenguinShape.fly()` breaks. Redesign — probably `Bird`, `Flying`, and `SwimmingBird` are separate concerns.

**Why:** LSP violations are subtle. Spotting them is a senior skill.
## Interview Q&A — with full answers you can quote

**Q1: Name the SOLID principles and give an example of violating each.**

**A:** **S — Single Responsibility:** a `UserManager` that validates, saves to DB, sends emails, and generates reports has four reasons to change. Fix: split into four classes. **O — Open/Closed:** an `area(shape)` function with an if/else on shape type — every new shape means editing existing code. Fix: use polymorphism so shapes provide their own area. **L — Liskov Substitution:** `Square extends Rectangle` — setting width also changes height, breaking any caller that expected independent axes. Fix: don't force the inheritance. **I — Interface Segregation:** a `Printer` interface with `print()`, `scan()`, and `fax()` forces a basic printer to implement scan and fax as no-ops. Fix: split into `Printable`, `Scannable`, `Faxable`. **D — Dependency Inversion:** an `OrderService` that news up a `SqlRepository` directly — can't be tested without a real DB. Fix: depend on an abstract `Repository` interface and inject the concrete class.

**Q2: Explain the Square/Rectangle LSP violation and why it matters.**

**A:** Geometrically, a square 'is a' rectangle — same corners, same right angles. Modeled naively: `class Square extends Rectangle` where `setWidth` also sets `setHeight` to keep it square. But now any function `void resize(Rectangle r) { r.setWidth(3); r.setHeight(4); assert r.area() == 12; }` fails when given a Square — because setWidth silently set the height too. The Square silently violates the Rectangle's contract. **Liskov** says a subclass must be a genuine substitute for its parent — not just field-compatible but behaviorally compatible. Fix: don't make Square a subclass of Rectangle — model them separately, or use an immutable `Shape` interface. The lesson: 'is-a' in code must include behavior, not just names.

**Q3: How does Dependency Inversion enable unit testing?**

**A:** Without DIP, a class creates its own dependencies — `class OrderService { final SqlRepo repo = SqlRepo(); }`. To test it, you'd need a real SQL database. With DIP, the class receives its dependencies through constructor parameters typed as abstractions — `class OrderService { final Repo repo; OrderService(this.repo); }`. In production you inject `SqlRepo`; in tests you inject a `FakeRepo` that just stores data in a Map. Now `OrderService`'s logic can be tested in milliseconds without touching a database, network, or filesystem. Every unit-testing framework (JUnit, Mockito, mocktail, fake_async) exists to make this pattern smooth. In the industry, **DIP + constructor injection is table stakes for testable code**.

**Q4: What's the difference between Dependency Inversion and Dependency Injection?**

**A:** **Dependency Inversion (the principle)** says high-level modules should depend on abstractions, and abstractions should not depend on concrete details. It's a *design principle* about the direction of the dependency arrow. **Dependency Injection (the technique)** is one common way to achieve it — the concrete implementation is passed into a class from the outside (via constructor, setter, or interface). You can follow DIP without a framework — just constructor parameters typed as interfaces. Frameworks (Spring, Hilt, Riverpod, get_it) automate the wiring so you don't hand-instantiate objects at every layer. In short: DIP is the *why*, DI is the *how*.

**Q5: How do SOLID principles relate to Clean Architecture?**

**A:** Clean Architecture is essentially SOLID applied at the architectural level. The dependency rule — outer layers depend on inner layers, never the reverse — is Dependency Inversion writ large. Interface Segregation shows up as ports/adapters: each outer service depends on a small port. Single Responsibility appears as each layer having one job (domain = business rules, infrastructure = adapters, delivery = UI). Open/Closed shows up in swapping infrastructure implementations (Postgres vs Firestore) without touching business rules. Liskov shows up in adapters being genuine substitutes for their port interfaces. If you deeply understand SOLID, Clean Architecture stops feeling like a religion and starts feeling like the natural conclusion of applying the principles to a whole application.

**Q6: When would you NOT apply a SOLID principle?**

**A:** When applying it would make the code *worse*. Examples: creating an interface for a class with only one implementation adds ceremony without benefit — YAGNI (you aren't gonna need it). Splitting a small cohesive class into three tiny classes because 'SRP' fragments code and hurts readability. Adding a strategy pattern when a single `if` statement is clearer. The principles exist to make change safer and code more understandable — if they don't, they're not helping. Real seniors apply SOLID *pragmatically*: start simple, refactor toward SOLID when actual pain (hard to test, hard to change) shows up. Applying every principle to every class from day one is called over-engineering, and it's how you get 100-file codebases for what should be a 5-file feature.

**Q7: Give an example of applying Open/Closed with polymorphism.**

**A:** Consider a discount system that switches on `discountType`: `if (type == 'PERCENT') return price * (1 - value/100); else if (type == 'FIXED') return price - value; ...`. Every new discount type means editing this method — the *closed* part fails. Refactor to polymorphism: `abstract class Discount { double apply(double price); }`. Concrete subclasses: `PercentDiscount`, `FixedDiscount`, `BuyOneGetOneDiscount`. The pricing method becomes `discounts.fold(price, (p, d) => d.apply(p))`. Adding a new discount type = adding a class. **Zero changes to the pricing function.** That's OCP: open for extension (new subclasses), closed for modification (no edits to existing code).

## Real-world examples

- **Spring (Java)** — the entire framework is DIP. You `@Autowired` an interface; Spring picks the implementation. Swap by config.
- **Flutter's `get_it` and Riverpod** — DIP in Dart. Providers hand your widgets abstract dependencies; you inject fakes in widget tests.
- **Every payment gateway integration** — you code against an abstract `PaymentGateway`, and each concrete integration (Stripe, Razorpay, PayU) is a subclass. Adding a new gateway is one class, zero changes to callers. That's OCP + DIP together.
- **Every driver architecture** (JDBC, ODBC) is Interface Segregation — the driver interface only exposes what any DB must do; vendor-specific features live in separate optional interfaces.

## Advanced notes

- **The Stable Dependencies Principle** — packages should depend in the direction of stability. Volatile packages should depend on stable ones, not vice versa.
- **The Common Closure Principle** — classes that change together should live together. The package-level analog of SRP.
- **Hexagonal Architecture (Ports & Adapters)** — a specific realization of SOLID for whole applications. Domain is at the center; adapters plug in at the edges.
- **The pit-of-success principle** — good SOLID makes correct code the easy path, incorrect code the hard path.
- **SOLID vs GRASP** — GRASP (General Responsibility Assignment Software Patterns) is a lesser-known but rich set of complementary principles worth studying if you like this stuff.

flashcards.back: Single Responsibility · Open/Closed · Liskov Substitution · Interface Segregation · Dependency Inversion.
A class should have only one reason to change.
Open for extension, closed for modification. Add new behavior without editing existing code.
A subclass must be substitutable for its parent without callers noticing.
Depend on abstractions (interfaces), not concrete classes. Inject the concrete class from outside.

quiz.q: A class doing four unrelated jobs violates…
quiz.explain: Single Responsibility Principle — should have one reason to change.

quiz.q: Square extends Rectangle and breaks callers — violates…
quiz.explain: Liskov Substitution — subclass doesn't behave like parent.

quiz.q: One 'Printer' interface with print, scan, and fax methods violates…
quiz.explain: Interface Segregation — many small interfaces beat one fat one.

quiz.q: Adding a new discount type without editing pricing logic is…

challenges.text: Take a God class and split it into single-responsibility pieces.
Convert an if/else-on-type function to polymorphism (OCP).
Introduce a Repository interface and inject it into a service. Write a fake for tests.
Split a fat interface into three focused ones and refactor implementations.
Find a Liskov violation in existing code and fix it via composition or a shared interface.
Refactor the NotificationService sample in this topic — end-to-end, with tests.
Apply all five principles to a small feature end-to-end (e.g. a Flutter feature: UI, ViewModel, Repository, DataSource). Document each application.

What's the difference between Dependency Inversion and Dependency Injection?
How do SOLID principles relate to Clean Architecture?
When would you NOT apply a SOLID principle?
Give an example of applying Open/Closed with polymorphism.
